Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: We are given an event log with columns case_id activity timestamp We need to find cases that have a specific sequence of three activities in direct succession Approve Purchase Requisition Create Order Confirm Order And then in that sequence the time between Create Order and Confirm Order is more than 5 days

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Steps 1 Identify the events that are part of the sequence in direct succession We can use window functions to get the next activity and the next next activity within the same case ordered by timestamp

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: 2 We then look for a pattern where activity Approve Purchase Requisition next_activity Create Order next_next_activity Confirm Order

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: 3 For the matched sequence we also need to check the time between the Create Order and Confirm Order events Note We are only interested in the sequence that matches exactly and then we check the time between the second and third event

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: 4 We then get the case_ids that have at least one such sequence with the time condition more than 5 days between Create Order and Confirm Order

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: 5 Finally we want to exclude events from these case_ids

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Approach We can do a Use window functions to assign row numbers and get the next activity and timestamp for the same case b Filter for the sequence and then check the time condition

Name: Validation - PE
Text: However note that we are only interested in the exact sequence in direct succession meaning that there are no other events between them

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We can use LEAD activity 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_activity LEAD activity 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_next_activity LEAD timestamp 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_timestamp LEAD timestamp 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_next_timestamp

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then for an event that is the first in the sequence the Approve Purchase Requisition we check next_activity Create Order and next_next_activity Confirm Order and then check the time difference between next_timestamp and next_next_timestamp which are the timestamps of Create Order and Confirm Order

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Alternatively we can use three self joins or window functions But the window function approach is straightforward

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Lets do WITH sequenced_events AS SELECT case_id activity timestamp LEAD activity 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_activity LEAD activity 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_next_activity LEAD timestamp 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_timestamp LEAD timestamp 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_next_timestamp FROM event_log cases_to_exclude AS SELECT DISTINCT case_id FROM sequenced_events WHERE activity Approve Purchase Requisition AND next_activity Create Order AND next_next_activity Confirm Order AND DATEDIFF day next_timestamp next_next_timestamp greater than 5 SELECT e FROM event_log e WHERE e case_id NOT IN SELECT case_id FROM cases_to_exclude

Name: Validation - PE
Text: However note The DATEDIFF function in DuckDB DATEDIFF unit start end returns the difference in the given unit We want more than 5 days so DATEDIFF day next_timestamp next_next_timestamp greater than 5

Name: Validation - PE
Text: But note The condition is on the time between Create Order and Confirm Order which are the next_timestamp and next_next_timestamp in our window

Name: Validation - IND
Text: However we must be cautious The window function is applied to each row and we are only looking at the row that is the first in the sequence

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Alternatively we can also use a self join to find the three consecutive events and then check the time condition

Name: Validation - PE
Text: But note We are only interested in the first activity of the sequence We are using the window function to look ahead two rows

Name: Validation - PE
Text: However what if there are multiple sequences in the same case We are using DISTINCT case_id in the subquery so if a case has multiple such sequences it will be excluded This is correct because if a case has at least one such sequence with the time condition we want to exclude the entire case

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Lets adjust the query to also consider that we are only interested in the sequence of three in direct succession meaning that the events are consecutive in the case The window function approach is correct because it orders by timestamp and then the next two rows are the next two events in the case

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We are using LEAD with offsets 1 and 2 We are filtering for the first activity of the sequence and then checking the next two We must also ensure that the next and next_next activities are exactly the ones we want and that the time condition is met

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Let me run a test in my mind Case 1 Event1 Approve Purchase Requisition t1 Event2 Create Order t2 Event3 Confirm Order t3 For Event1 the window function gives next_activity Create Order next_next_activity Confirm Order next_timestamp t2 next_next_timestamp t3 Then we check DATEDIFF day t2 t3 greater than 5 This is correct

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - PE
Text: But what if the sequence is not exactly in three rows For example if there are two Approve Purchase Requisition events in a row Then the window function would still look at the next two rows which might not be the correct sequence

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Example Event1 Approve Purchase Requisition t1 Event2 Approve Purchase Requisition t2 Event3 Create Order t3 Event4 Confirm Order t4 For Event1 the window function gives next_activity Approve Purchase Requisition not matching For Event2 the window function gives next_activity Create Order next_next_activity Confirm Order So Event2 would be the start of the sequence This is correct because the sequence must be in direct succession Event2 is the last of the two Approve Purchase Requisition and then we have the sequence

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: So the window function approach works We can write the query as WITH sequenced_events AS SELECT case_id activity timestamp LEAD activity 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_activity LEAD activity 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_next_activity LEAD timestamp 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_timestamp LEAD timestamp 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp as next_next_timestamp FROM event_log cases_to_exclude AS SELECT DISTINCT case_id FROM sequenced_events WHERE activity Approve Purchase Requisition AND next_activity Create Order AND next_next_activity Confirm Order AND DATEDIFF day next_timestamp next_next_timestamp greater than 5 SELECT e FROM event_log e WHERE e case_id NOT IN SELECT case_id FROM cases_to_exclude

Name: Validation - PE
Text: However note The condition is on the time between the Create Order and Confirm Order events In the window we have next_timestamp timestamp of Create Order next_next_timestamp timestamp of Confirm Order We are using DATEDIFF day next_timestamp next_next_timestamp greater than 5 This should work

Name: Validation - PE
Text: But wait What if the sequence is not in the order of the window We are ordering by timestamp so the events are in chronological order We are also partitioning by case_id so we are looking within the same case This should be correct

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Let me think about edge cases What if there are multiple sequences in the same case We are using DISTINCT so the case will be excluded What if the sequence is at the end of the case Then the LEAD for the next_next might be NULL for the last two events We are only interested in the first event of the sequence and if the sequence is complete then the next_next is not NULL So we are safe What if the case has less than 3 events Then the LEAD will return NULL for the next_next and the condition will fail so the case wont be excluded

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We are only excluding cases that have the sequence with the time condition This should meet the requirements

Name: Validation - PE
Text: However note The problem says direct succession meaning that there are no other events between the three Our window function does exactly that because we are looking at the next two events in the order of timestamps We are not skipping any events So the query is as above Let me write it clearly
